court: High Court of Judicature at Madras
facts: The plaintiff (Chinnasamy) was a tenant of an agricultural property and on 01.05.1992 entered into Ext.A.1 agreement to purchase the property for Rs.75,000, having paid Rs.50,000 and undertaking to pay the balance Rs.25,000 within 11 months; he was given possession. Defendants 1 to 3 allegedly attempted to create documents in favour of the fourth defendant, and on 24.09.1992 the second defendant executed Ext.B.1 sale deed in favour of the fourth defendant. At trial the court found Ext.A.1 genuine and decreed specific performance; the first appellate court, while also finding Ext.A.1 genuine, dismissed the suit holding the plaintiff was not ready and willing to perform. The plaintiff appealed to this High Court which dismissed the second appeal and upheld the appellate court's judgment.
arguments_claimant: The plaintiff argued that Ext.A.1 was a genuine sale agreement and that the fourth defendant failed to plead that he was a bonafide purchaser for value without notice under Section 19(b) of the Specific Relief Act, citing R.K. Mohammed Ubaidullah. He contended that because the fourth defendant omitted the plea, the plaintiff should not have to bear the burden of proving notice and that the trial court's decree should be restored.
arguments_respondent: The respondents (LRs of the fourth defendant) argued that the plaintiff failed to plead or prove readiness and willingness to perform his part of the contract under Section 16(C) of the Specific Relief Act and that tenancy was not proved. They also argued there was nothing on record to indicate the fourth defendant had notice of Ext.A.1 and relied on Padmakumari v. Dasayyan.
ratio_decidendi: "The decision to this case is rooted in the fundamental guiding principal that a decree for specific performances [is] a discretionary relief, and that he who claims specific enforcement of an agreement should satisfy the conscience of the court that he deserves it." The Court held that "the burden is entirely on the plaintiff to establish that the failure of the fourth defendant to plead that he was a bonafide purchaser for value without notice of Ext.A.1 cannot ipso facto discharge the burden on the plaintiff to prove that the fourth defendant had notice of Ext.A.1, though it may be counted as a circumstance." The Court also emphasized that "he has not pleaded that he was ready and willing to perform his part of the contract in terms of Section 16(C) of the Specific Relief Act" and that Section 16(C) "mandates that the party seeking specific enforcement shall plead and prove his readiness and willingness to perform his part of the contract." Finally, the Court held that because specific performance is equitable relief "the plaintiff still may require to prove that the fourth defendant had notice of Ext.A.1" and thus must "walk the extra distance to satisfy the Court's conscience that he is entitled to obtain a decree for specific performance."
final_order: The second appeal is dismissed and the judgment and decree dated 12.07.2000 passed in A.S.No.34 of 1999 is upheld; no costs.
summary: This is a High Court appeal in a suit for specific performance of a sale agreement (Ext.A.1 dated 01.05.1992) for agricultural land. The trial court decreed specific performance, but the first appellate court reversed, finding the plaintiff not ready and willing to perform; the High Court agreed and dismissed the second appeal. The court stressed that specific performance is discretionary, the plaintiff must plead and prove readiness and willingness (Section 16(C) Specific Relief Act), and must prove notice to a subsequent purchaser even if that purchaser did not plead the bona fide purchaser exception under Section 19(b). Practical effect: failure to prove readiness/willingness and notice will defeat specific performance even where the agreement is found genuine.
embedding_summary: Chinnasamy v. K. Viswanathan (dead), S.A.No.1736 of 2000, High Court of Madras, judgment dated 12.03.2020. Plaintiff (tenant) executed Ext.A.1 sale agreement on 01.05.1992 for Rs.75,000 (Rs.50,000 paid; Rs.25,000 balance in 11 months) and got possession; defendants executed Ext.B.1 sale deed on 24.09.1992 in favour of fourth defendant. Trial Court found Ext.A.1 genuine and decreed specific performance; first appellate court found plaintiff not "ready and willing" and dismissed; High Court dismissed second appeal. Key legal issues: Section 19(b) Specific Relief Act (bona fide purchaser for value without notice), Section 16(C) Specific Relief Act (pleading/proof of readiness and willingness), discretionary nature of specific performance, onus of proof of notice, evidence under Section 73 Evidence Act. Authorities relied upon: R.K. Mohammed Ubaidullah v. Hajee C. Abdul Wahab, (2000) 6 SCC 402; Padmakumari v. Dasayyan, (2015) 8 SCC 695. Outcome: appeal dismissed; no monetary award.
legal_principles: Specific performance is a discretionary equitable relief; claimant must satisfy the court's conscience before decree is granted.
Section 16(C) Specific Relief Act requires the plaintiff to plead and prove readiness and willingness to perform as a statutory burden.
Section 19(b) Specific Relief Act's bona fide purchaser exception is a plea onus of the purchaser, but the plaintiff still must prove notice to obtain equitable relief.
Failure of a subsequent purchaser to plead bona fide purchaser status may be a circumstance but does not ipso facto relieve the plaintiff of proving notice.
When seeking specific performance against a subsequent purchaser, the plaintiff must go the 'extra distance' in equity to prove entitlement.
sections_cited: Section 19(b) of the Specific Relief Act
Section 16(C) of the Specific Relief Act
cases_cited: R.K. Mohammed Ubaidullah & Ors v Hajee C. Abdul Wahab (D) & Ors, (2000) 6 SCC 402
Padmakumari & Ors v Dasayyan & Others, (2015) 8 SCC 695
bm25_text: Chinnasamy vs K.Viswanathan(Died) on 12 March, 2020 High Court of Judicature at Madras 2020 The plaintiff (Chinnasamy) was a tenant of an agricultural property and on 01.05.1992 entered into Ext.A.1 agreement to purchase the property for Rs.75,000, having paid Rs.50,000 and undertaking to pay the balance Rs.25,000 within 11 months; he was given possession. Defendants 1 to 3 allegedly attempted to create documents in favour of the fourth defendant, and on 24.09.1992 the second defendant executed Ext.B.1 sale deed in favour of the fourth defendant. At trial the court found Ext.A.1 genuine and decreed specific performance; the first appellate court, while also finding Ext.A.1 genuine, dismissed the suit holding the plaintiff was not ready and willing to perform. The plaintiff appealed to this High Court which dismissed the second appeal and upheld the appellate court's judgment. The plaintiff argued that Ext.A.1 was a genuine sale agreement and that the fourth defendant failed to plead that he was a bonafide purchaser for value without notice under Section 19(b) of the Specific Relief Act, citing R.K. Mohammed Ubaidullah. He contended that because the fourth defendant omitted the plea, the plaintiff should not have to bear the burden of proving notice and that the trial court's decree should be restored. The respondents (LRs of the fourth defendant) argued that the plaintiff failed to plead or prove readiness and willingness to perform his part of the contract under Section 16(C) of the Specific Relief Act and that tenancy was not proved. They also argued there was nothing on record to indicate the fourth defendant had notice of Ext.A.1 and relied on Padmakumari v. Dasayyan. "The decision to this case is rooted in the fundamental guiding principal that a decree for specific performances [is] a discretionary relief, and that he who claims specific enforcement of an agreement should satisfy the conscience of the court that he deserves it." The Court held that "the burden is entirely on the plaintiff to establish that the failure of the fourth defendant to plead that he was a bonafide purchaser for value without notice of Ext.A.1 cannot ipso facto discharge the burden on the plaintiff to prove that the fourth defendant had notice of Ext.A.1, though it may be counted as a circumstance." The Court also emphasized that "he has not pleaded that he was ready and willing to perform his part of the contract in terms of Section 16(C) of the Specific Relief Act" and that Section 16(C) "mandates that the party seeking specific enforcement shall plead and prove his readiness and willingness to perform his part of the contract." Finally, the Court held that because specific performance is equitable relief "the plaintiff still may require to prove that the fourth defendant had notice of Ext.A.1" and thus must "walk the extra distance to satisfy the Court's conscience that he is entitled to obtain a decree for specific performance." The second appeal is dismissed and the judgment and decree dated 12.07.2000 passed in A.S.No.34 of 1999 is upheld; no costs. The court stressed that specific performance is discretionary, the plaintiff must plead and prove readiness and willingness (Section 16(C) Specific Relief Act), and must prove notice to a subsequent purchaser even if that purchaser did not plead the bona fide purchaser exception under Section 19(b). Practical effect: failure to prove readiness/willingness and notice will defeat specific performance even where the agreement is found genuine. Chinnasamy v. K. Section 16(C) Specific Relief Act requires the plaintiff to plead and prove readiness and willingness to perform as a statutory burden. Section 19(b) Specific Relief Act's bona fide purchaser exception is a plea onus of the purchaser, but the plaintiff still must prove notice to obtain equitable relief.
